mnemonics.title: PAPER Prototype Benefits
mnemonics.content: P - Prototyping (Rapid), A - Affordable, P - Participatory, E - Early Testing, R - Revolutionary (for iterations)

mnemonics.title: Wizard of Oz Process
mnemonics.content: W - Welcome user, I - Introduce task, Z - Observe silently, A - Act as system behind scenes, R - Record issues, D - Document feedback, O - Organize findings, Z - Zero in on improvements

acronyms.usage: Use LOW-HIGH progression: Start with paper prototypes, then move to digital mockups after validating core interactions
Remember FAID when prototyping: Fidelity determines feedback type, Abstraction encourages honesty, Iteration improves design, Documentation captures learning

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a flipbook where each page is a screen - flip through pages to navigate between screens in your prototype
Think of a puppeteer behind a curtain - the 'wizard' pulls the strings (responds to user actions) to make the paper prototype come alive
Horizontal = wide but shallow lake; Vertical = narrow but deep well - both represent different depths of design exploration
Think of a spiral: Sketch → Test → Analyze → Redesign → Repeat - each cycle gets closer to the center (perfect solution)
Low-fidelity = sketch on napkin; High-fidelity = finished painting - same content, different level of detail

analogies.analogy: Like architectural blueprints - rough drawings that convey the essential structure before building begins
Like a human-powered vending machine - someone inside manually dispenses items while you think the machine works automatically
Like clay sculpting - you can quickly shape and reshape, unlike carving stone (digital) which requires more commitment
Like building IKEA furniture together with a friend - collaboration makes the process better and you both learn from each other
Like movie pre-production: storyboards (low-fi) → animatics (med-fi) → final animation (high-fi) - each stage serves different purposes
